01_initial_request.input_data: Superalloy workpieces, drilling tools, and machining conditions
01_initial_request.user_intent: To propose a multi-objective optimization strategy for continuous drilling process parameters to balance manufacturing efficiency, cost, and machining quality
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Maximize tool life and minimize machining energy consumption

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Superalloy aeroengine components require continuous drilling with multiple holes, but face challenges of high tool costs and low energy efficiency. [Gap] No unified mathematical model exists for multi-objective optimization of continuous drilling parameters considering both tool life and energy consumption. [Decision] To achieve parameter optimization, chose to use mathematical modeling to establish optimization problem formulation with objective functions and constraints on superalloy drilling process, expecting to obtain formal optimization framework.

constraints: machine tool rated power consumption constraint

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Established unified mathematical model for multi-objective optimization of drilling parameters with objective functions and constraint conditions defined in equations (1)-(12)

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Tool life directly affects processing cost and energy efficiency in continuous drilling. [Gap] Need predictive model relating tool wear to machining parameters for optimization. [Decision] To achieve tool life maximization, chose to use power law model to establish tool life prediction model on drilling parameters, expecting to obtain tool wear function VB(N) and drilling length L.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Established tool life model expressing drilling length L as function of spindle speed n, feed f, and drilling quantity N with equations (13)-(18)

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Processing energy consumption is critical for efficient manufacturing. [Gap] Need model relating energy consumption to cutting parameters and tool wear. [Decision] To achieve energy minimization, chose to use power consumption modeling to establish machining energy consumption model on drilling process, expecting to obtain total energy consumption E_total function.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Established energy consumption model with total power consumption expressed in equations (19)-(25) considering tool wear effects

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Models require experimental data for calibration and validation. [Gap] Need empirical data from actual drilling experiments on superalloy materials. [Decision] To achieve model calibration, chose to use CNC machine tool with carbide drill to perform drilling experiments on GH4169 superalloy plate, expecting to obtain drilling force and power data under different parameters.

parameters.drill_tool: 10 mm diameter carbide twist drill (GD03-1000, ZCC CT)
parameters.test_sets: 12 sets of full factors drilling tests

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Obtained drilling force and drilling power data under different cutting parameters during continuous drilling until tool life end

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Tool wear measurement data collected from experiments. [Gap] Need to calibrate tool life prediction model coefficients. [Decision] To achieve accurate tool life prediction, chose to use least squares fitting to perform model calibration on tool wear data, expecting to obtain model coefficients a0, a1, b0, b1.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Obtained model coefficients shown in Table 3 with tool wear rate prediction error below 10% (average 5.00%) and initial wear prediction error below 20% (average 10.45%)

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Energy consumption data collected from experiments. [Gap] Need to calibrate energy consumption prediction model. [Decision] To achieve accurate energy prediction, chose to use calibration experiments to perform power consumption model fitting on drilling data, expecting to obtain power consumption coefficients.

parameters.force_coefficients: ΔK_zw and ΔK_tw considering tool wear

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Obtained power consumption prediction model with average prediction error of 0.99% as shown in Table 7

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Both tool life and energy consumption models are calibrated. [Gap] Need to find optimal parameters balancing both objectives. [Decision] To achieve multi-objective optimization, chose to use NSGA-II algorithm to perform Pareto optimization on drilling parameters, expecting to obtain Pareto front solution set.

parameters.parameter_ranges: n ∈ [550 rpm, 850 rpm], f ∈ [44 mm/min, 144 mm/min]

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Obtained Pareto solution set containing 60 solutions with Pareto front showing trade-off between energy consumption E and drilling length L as shown in Fig. 17

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Optimization strategy developed with Pareto solutions. [Gap] Need experimental verification of optimization effectiveness. [Decision] To achieve strategy validation, chose to use drilling experiments to perform verification tests on optimized parameters, expecting to confirm prediction accuracy and optimization benefits.

parameters.verification_parameter_sets: 3 sets from Pareto intervals (tool life priority, balance, energy priority)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Confirmed prediction errors: energy consumption 5.86%, 1.57%, 4.51% (all <6%); tool wear 12.88%, 6.54%, 14.97% (all <15%). Optimized parameters increased total drilling length by 40% compared to non-optimal parameters

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Experimental verification through drilling tests comparing optimized vs non-optimized parameters with measurement of tool wear, energy consumption, and drilling length

tool_life_improvement.interpretation: Optimized machining parameters increase total drilling length by 40% compared to arbitrarily selected parameters

energy_consumption_reduction.interpretation: Difference between minimum (2.83×10^6 J) and maximum (5.35×10^6 J) energy consumption for same drilling length

tool_cost_reduction_single_parameter.interpretation: Tool cost reduction when optimizing feed rate at fixed spindle speed (550 rpm)

tool_cost_reduction_alternative.interpretation: Tool cost reduction when optimizing spindle speed at fixed feed rate (144 mm/min)

energy_prediction_accuracy.interpretation: Average prediction error of power consumption model

tool_life_prediction_accuracy.interpretation: Average error of tool life prediction model

verification_energy_error.interpretation: Prediction errors for three verification parameter sets, all below 6%

verification_wear_error.interpretation: Tool wear prediction errors for three verification sets, all below 15%

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The multi-objective optimization strategy successfully achieved the research objective. Experimental verification confirmed that the proposed strategy can significantly reduce drilling processing energy consumption (up to 52.8% difference between optimal and non-optimal) and increase the service life of drilling tools (40% improvement in drilling length). All prediction models demonstrated acceptable accuracy with energy consumption errors below 6% and tool wear errors below 15%.
